description: 10 pre-1940 public land historical sites suitable for metal detecting

locations.name: Battle of Honey Springs Site
locations.description: Largest Civil War battle in Indian Territory fought on July 17, 1863. Union forces under General Blunt defeated Confederate troops, securing control of Indian Territory. Now a state historic site with public access.
locations.notes: Honey Springs Battlefield Historic Site - Oklahoma state historic site, public access, metal detecting allowed with permit

locations.name: Fort Gibson Historic Site
locations.description: Established in 1824 as the westernmost U.S. military post, Fort Gibson was a key outpost during Cherokee removal and the Civil War. Served as terminus of the Trail of Tears for the Five Civilized Tribes.
locations.notes: Fort Gibson Historic Site - Oklahoma state historic site, open to public, museum and grounds accessible

locations.name: Fort Washita Historic Site
locations.description: Built in 1842 by General Zachary Taylor to protect Chickasaw and Choctaw lands from Plains Indian raids. Later used as a Confederate post during the Civil War. Ruins and grounds open to the public.
locations.notes: Fort Washita Historic Site - Oklahoma state historic site, public access, extensive grounds

locations.name: Black Mesa Cimarron Route Campsite
locations.description: Historic campsite area at Black Mesa on the Cimarron Cutoff of the Santa Fe Trail in Oklahoma's Panhandle (Cimarron County). Traders and military expeditions following the Cimarron Route camped in this area from the 1820s onward. The mesa and surrounding high plains are managed by the Bureau of Land Management. Highest point in Oklahoma at 4,973 feet, with documented trail ruts and historic campfire rings.
locations.source: BLM Oklahoma / National Santa Fe Trail Association - Public Land
locations.notes: Black Mesa BLM area - Bureau of Land Management managed, open to public, Cimarron County, OK - metal detecting allowed on BLM land

locations.name: Fort Supply Historic Site
locations.description: Established in November 1868 as Camp Supply to support General Sheridan's winter campaign against Southern Plains tribes. Served as a major military supply depot. The original stockade and several historic buildings survive.
locations.notes: Fort Supply Historic Site - Oklahoma state historic site, open to public

locations.name: Doaksville Ghost Town Site
locations.description: Founded in 1824 as a trading post, Doaksville became the capital of the Choctaw Nation and one of the largest towns in Indian Territory before the Civil War. Confederate General Stand Watie surrendered here in June 1865.
locations.notes: On public land near Fort Towson Historic Site - accessible via state historic site grounds

locations.name: Fort Towson Historic Site
locations.description: Established in 1824 to maintain peace between Choctaw settlers and Texans along the Red River. Site of the last Confederate surrender of the Civil War in June 1865. Ruins and grounds are publicly accessible.
locations.notes: Fort Towson Historic Site - Oklahoma state historic site, public access

locations.name: Boggy Depot State Park
locations.description: Established around 1837 as a Choctaw settlement and waystation on the Butterfield Overland Mail route. Served as a major supply depot during the Civil War. Cemetery and remnants of the historic town are preserved in the state park.
locations.notes: Boggy Depot State Park - Oklahoma state park, public access, historic cemetery on site

locations.name: Council Oak Tree - Creek Nation Site
locations.description: Historic site where Creek Nation leaders met under a large oak tree after the Trail of Tears to rekindle the sacred fire and re-establish their nation in Indian Territory circa 1836. The council oak and surrounding land are publicly accessible in Tulsa.
locations.notes: Council Oak Park - City of Tulsa public park, Creek Nation historic site, open access

locations.name: Camp Napoleon Confederate Site
locations.description: Location of the Camp Napoleon Compact of 1865, signed May 26, 1865, between Confederate-allied tribal nations at the end of the Civil War. One of the last Confederate meetings in Indian Territory. Located on public land in Caddo County.
locations.notes: Camp Napoleon historic marker site - public roadside access, Caddo County
